---
title: "Leading XGBoost Model Testing File"
subtitle: "Train SIPP XGBoost model, score ACS, and compare target-like validation performance"
format:
  pdf:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    colorlinks: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

```{r}
# ============================================================================
# PURPOSE
# ============================================================================
# This file is the leading XGBoost prediction workflow. It trains the SIPP donor
# model, scores ACS, validates the target-like threshold choice, and exports the
# Stata-facing prediction file.
#
# Current official threshold rule:
# - Train the XGBoost model on the SIPP donor sample.
# - Define quartile thresholds using the target-like SIPP test sample only.
# - Export those target-like threshold groups to ACS using the downstream names:
#     gbm_undocu_p
#     gbm_undocu_q
#     gbm_high_prob
#     gbm_low_prob
#     gbm_high_recall
# ============================================================================

report_timestamp <- format(Sys.time(), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S %Z")
cat("Report rendered at:", report_timestamp, "\n")
```

```{r}
# Packages
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(xgboost)
library(caret)
library(pROC)
library(knitr)
```

```{r}
# ============================================================================
# PATHS AND FILE NAMES
# ============================================================================

if (Sys.info()["sysname"] == "Windows") {
  base_dir <- "G:/Shared drives/Undocu Research"
} else {
  base_dir <- "~/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-vsovero@ucr.edu/Shared drives/Undocu Research"
}

data_path <- file.path(base_dir, "Data")

sipp_file <- file.path(data_path, "(Step 1 output) Core_TM SIPP 2008 Wave 2.csv")
acs_file  <- file.path(data_path, "ACS Target Sample.csv")

# Official Stata-facing ACS prediction export
acs_prediction_export <- file.path(data_path, "ACS_SIPP_gbm.csv")

# Optional saved R objects for reproducibility
ml_output_path <- file.path(data_path, "ML Outputs")
dir.create(ml_output_path, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)

leading_xgb_model_file <- file.path(ml_output_path, "leading_xgb_model.rds")
leading_xgb_metadata_file <- file.path(ml_output_path, "leading_xgb_metadata.rds")
```

```{r}
# ============================================================================
# MODEL SETTINGS
# ============================================================================

seed <- 1

# XGBoost tuning grid.
# Keep this small enough for iterative testing. Larger grids can be tested later.
xgb_grid <- expand.grid(
  eta = c(0.01, 0.05, 0.10),
  max_depth = c(3, 4, 6),
  subsample = c(0.8, 1.0),
  colsample_bytree = c(0.8, 1.0)
)

xgb_nrounds <- 5000
xgb_nfold <- 5
xgb_early_stopping_rounds <- 50
```

```{r}
# ============================================================================
# MODEL FEATURES
# ============================================================================
# This mirrors the cleaned GBM file.
#
# Because the donor sample below is restricted to employed respondents, employed
# is intentionally not included as a predictor. The target-like validation sample
# still uses employed == 1 as part of the validation definition.
# ============================================================================

features <- c(
  "age",
  "fem",
  "married",
  "cit_spouse",
  "medicaid",
  "nonfluent",
  "spanish_hispanic_latino",
  "central_latino",
  "bpl_asia",
  "household_size",
  "poverty",
  "asian",
  "black",
  "white",
  "other_race",
  "years_us",
  "years_us_missing",
  "yrsed"
)
```

```{r}
# ============================================================================
# READ CLEANED SIPP AND ACS FILES
# ============================================================================

sipp_raw <- read_csv(sipp_file, show_col_types = FALSE)
acs_raw <- read_csv(acs_file, show_col_types = FALSE)

tibble(
  file = c("SIPP donor file", "ACS target file"),
  raw_rows = c(nrow(sipp_raw), nrow(acs_raw))
) %>%
  kable()
```

```{r}
# ============================================================================
# PREPARE MODEL SAMPLES
# ============================================================================
# EDIT THE SIPP FILTER HERE to test alternative donor samples.
# Current comparison donor sample: employed SIPP respondents ages 18-55.
# This matches the preferred cleaned GBM testing file.
# ============================================================================

sipp <- sipp_raw %>%
  mutate(
    # SIPP outcome may be stored as "X1", "1", or numeric 1 depending on export.
    undocu_likely = ifelse(undocu_likely %in% c("X1", "1", 1), 1, 0)
  ) %>%
  filter(
    age >= 18,
    age <= 55,
    employed==1
    # To test college-only donor training, add: , college == 1
    # To test age 22-55 donor training, change age >= 18 to age >= 22
  )

# Drop rows missing the outcome, any model feature, or college.
# college is retained because it defines the target-like SIPP validation sample.
sipp <- sipp[
  complete.cases(sipp[, c("undocu_likely", features, "college")]),
]

acs <- acs_raw[
  complete.cases(acs_raw[, c("year", "serial", "pernum", features)]),
]

tibble(
  sample = c("SIPP", "ACS"),
  raw_rows = c(nrow(sipp_raw), nrow(acs_raw)),
  model_rows = c(nrow(sipp), nrow(acs)),
  rows_dropped = c(nrow(sipp_raw) - nrow(sipp), nrow(acs_raw) - nrow(acs))
) %>%
  kable()
```

```{r}
# ============================================================================
# SPLIT SIPP INTO TRAINING AND TEST SAMPLES
# ============================================================================

set.seed(seed)

train_index <- createDataPartition(
  sipp$undocu_likely,
  p = 0.70,
  list = FALSE
)

train_xgb <- sipp[train_index, ]
test_xgb  <- sipp[-train_index, ]

tibble(
  sample = c("Training SIPP", "Held-out SIPP test"),
  n = c(nrow(train_xgb), nrow(test_xgb)),
  positives = c(
    sum(train_xgb$undocu_likely == 1),
    sum(test_xgb$undocu_likely == 1)
  ),
  positive_share = c(
    mean(train_xgb$undocu_likely == 1),
    mean(test_xgb$undocu_likely == 1)
  )
) %>%
  kable(digits = 4)
```

```{r}
# ============================================================================
# CREATE XGBOOST MATRICES
# ============================================================================
# XGBoost requires numeric matrices. The variables in features are already coded
# numerically, but mutate(across(..., as.numeric)) makes that explicit.
# ============================================================================

train_matrix <- train_xgb %>%
  select(all_of(features)) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), as.numeric)) %>%
  as.matrix()

test_matrix <- test_xgb %>%
  select(all_of(features)) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), as.numeric)) %>%
  as.matrix()

acs_matrix <- acs %>%
  select(all_of(features)) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), as.numeric)) %>%
  as.matrix()

train_y <- as.numeric(train_xgb$undocu_likely)
test_y <- as.numeric(test_xgb$undocu_likely)

dtrain <- xgb.DMatrix(data = train_matrix, label = train_y)
dtest  <- xgb.DMatrix(data = test_matrix, label = test_y)
dacs   <- xgb.DMatrix(data = acs_matrix)
```

```{r}
# ============================================================================
# TUNE XGBOOST MODEL USING CROSS-VALIDATED AUC
# ============================================================================
# For each grid row, xgb.cv chooses the best number of boosting rounds using
# held-out folds from the training sample. We then choose the grid row with the
# highest cross-validated test AUC.
# ============================================================================

xgb_grid$optimal_rounds <- NA_integer_
xgb_grid$cv_auc <- NA_real_

set.seed(seed)

for (i in 1:nrow(xgb_grid)) {
  params_i <- list(
    objective = "binary:logistic",
    eval_metric = "auc",
    eta = xgb_grid$eta[i],
    max_depth = xgb_grid$max_depth[i],
    subsample = xgb_grid$subsample[i],
    colsample_bytree = xgb_grid$colsample_bytree[i]
  )

  cv_i <- xgb.cv(
    params = params_i,
    data = dtrain,
    nrounds = xgb_nrounds,
    nfold = xgb_nfold,
    early_stopping_rounds = xgb_early_stopping_rounds,
    maximize = TRUE,
    verbose = 0
  )

  eval_log_i <- cv_i$evaluation_log

  xgb_grid$cv_auc[i] <- max(eval_log_i$test_auc_mean)
  xgb_grid$optimal_rounds[i] <- eval_log_i$iter[which.max(eval_log_i$test_auc_mean)]
}

xgb_grid <- xgb_grid %>%
  arrange(desc(cv_auc))

kable(xgb_grid, digits = 4)
```

```{r}
# ============================================================================
# TRAIN FINAL XGBOOST MODEL USING BEST GRID ROW
# ============================================================================

best_xgb <- xgb_grid[1, ]

final_params <- list(
  objective = "binary:logistic",
  eval_metric = "auc",
  eta = best_xgb$eta,
  max_depth = best_xgb$max_depth,
  subsample = best_xgb$subsample,
  colsample_bytree = best_xgb$colsample_bytree
)

set.seed(seed)

xgb_model <- xgb.train(
  params = final_params,
  data = dtrain,
  nrounds = best_xgb$optimal_rounds,
  verbose = 0
)

tibble(
  setting = c("eta", "max_depth", "subsample", "colsample_bytree", "optimal_rounds", "CV AUC"),
  value = c(
    best_xgb$eta,
    best_xgb$max_depth,
    best_xgb$subsample,
    best_xgb$colsample_bytree,
    best_xgb$optimal_rounds,
    best_xgb$cv_auc
  )
) %>%
  kable(digits = 4)
```

```{r}
# ============================================================================
# VARIABLE IMPORTANCE
# ============================================================================
# Gain is the most useful XGBoost importance metric for this purpose. It measures
# the relative contribution of splits using each variable.
# ============================================================================

xgb_importance <- xgb.importance(
  feature_names = features,
  model = xgb_model
)

kable(xgb_importance, digits = 4)
```

```{r}
# ============================================================================
# PREDICT PROBABILITIES IN SIPP TEST AND ACS TARGET SAMPLES
# ============================================================================

test_xgb$gbm_undocu_p <- predict(xgb_model, newdata = dtest)
acs$gbm_undocu_p <- predict(xgb_model, newdata = dacs)

bind_rows(
  test_xgb %>%
    summarise(
      sample = "Held-out SIPP test",
      n = n(),
      mean_probability = mean(gbm_undocu_p),
      p25 = quantile(gbm_undocu_p, .25),
      median = median(gbm_undocu_p),
      p75 = quantile(gbm_undocu_p, .75)
    ),
  acs %>%
    summarise(
      sample = "ACS target",
      n = n(),
      mean_probability = mean(gbm_undocu_p),
      p25 = quantile(gbm_undocu_p, .25),
      median = median(gbm_undocu_p),
      p75 = quantile(gbm_undocu_p, .75)
    )
) %>%
  kable(digits = 4)
```

```{r}
# ============================================================================
# DEFINE COMPARISON QUARTILES USING TARGET-LIKE SIPP TEST SAMPLE
# ============================================================================
# Threshold reference sample: SIPP test observations that match the ACS target
# population: college graduates, employed, ages 22-55.
# ============================================================================

sipp_targetlike_test <- test_xgb %>%
  filter(
    college == 1,
    employed == 1,
    age >= 22,
    age <= 55
  )

xgb_targetlike_breaks <- quantile(
  sipp_targetlike_test$gbm_undocu_p,
  probs = c(0, .25, .50, .75, 1),
  na.rm = TRUE
)

xgb_targetlike_breaks[1] <- -Inf
xgb_targetlike_breaks[5] <- Inf

sipp_targetlike_test$gbm_undocu_q <- cut(
  sipp_targetlike_test$gbm_undocu_p,
  breaks = xgb_targetlike_breaks,
  include.lowest = TRUE,
  labels = c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4")
)

acs$gbm_undocu_q <- cut(
  acs$gbm_undocu_p,
  breaks = xgb_targetlike_breaks,
  include.lowest = TRUE,
  labels = c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4")
)

tibble(
  cutoff = c("Q1/Q2", "Q2/Q3", "Q3/Q4"),
  probability_cutoff = c(
    xgb_targetlike_breaks[2],
    xgb_targetlike_breaks[3],
    xgb_targetlike_breaks[4]
  )
) %>%
  kable(digits = 4)

xgb_targetlike_sipp_quartiles <- sipp_targetlike_test %>%
  group_by(gbm_undocu_q) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    positives = sum(undocu_likely == 1),
    precision = mean(undocu_likely == 1),
    mean_probability = mean(gbm_undocu_p),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

xgb_acs_quartiles <- acs %>%
  group_by(gbm_undocu_q) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    share = n() / nrow(acs),
    mean_probability = mean(gbm_undocu_p),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

kable(xgb_targetlike_sipp_quartiles, digits = 4)
kable(xgb_acs_quartiles, digits = 4)
```

```{r}
# ============================================================================
# DEFINE HIGH-RECALL THRESHOLD USING TARGET-LIKE SIPP TEST SAMPLE
# ============================================================================
# This threshold mirrors the cleaned GBM file: it is the lowest predicted
# probability among the top 75% of true positives in the target-like SIPP test
# sample.
# ============================================================================

top_75_targetlike <- sipp_targetlike_test %>%
  filter(undocu_likely == 1) %>%
  arrange(desc(gbm_undocu_p)) %>%
  mutate(
    rank = row_number(),
    cumulative_share_of_positives = rank / n(),
    top_75 = cumulative_share_of_positives <= 0.75
  ) %>%
  filter(top_75 == TRUE)

threshold_75_xgb <- min(top_75_targetlike$gbm_undocu_p)

tibble(
  threshold = "Target-like high recall",
  probability_cutoff = threshold_75_xgb
) %>%
  kable(digits = 4)
```

```{r}
# ============================================================================
# MODEL VALIDATION: AUC AND LIFT
# ============================================================================

auc_full <- pROC::auc(
  pROC::roc(
    response = test_xgb$undocu_likely,
    predictor = test_xgb$gbm_undocu_p,
    quiet = TRUE
  )
)

auc_targetlike <- pROC::auc(
  pROC::roc(
    response = sipp_targetlike_test$undocu_likely,
    predictor = sipp_targetlike_test$gbm_undocu_p,
    quiet = TRUE
  )
)

tibble(
  validation_sample = c("Full SIPP test", "Target-like SIPP test"),
  auc = c(as.numeric(auc_full), as.numeric(auc_targetlike))
) %>%
  kable(digits = 4)

targetlike_base_rate <- mean(sipp_targetlike_test$undocu_likely == 1)

xgb_targetlike_lift <- xgb_targetlike_sipp_quartiles %>%
  mutate(
    base_rate = targetlike_base_rate,
    lift = precision / base_rate
  )

kable(xgb_targetlike_lift, digits = 4)
```

```{r}
# ============================================================================
# PROBABILITY DISTRIBUTION CHECK
# ============================================================================

prob_distribution_check <- bind_rows(
  test_xgb %>%
    mutate(sample = "Full SIPP test") %>%
    select(sample, gbm_undocu_p),
  sipp_targetlike_test %>%
    mutate(sample = "Target-like SIPP test") %>%
    select(sample, gbm_undocu_p),
  acs %>%
    mutate(sample = "ACS target") %>%
    select(sample, gbm_undocu_p)
) %>%
  group_by(sample) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_probability = mean(gbm_undocu_p),
    p10 = quantile(gbm_undocu_p, .10),
    p25 = quantile(gbm_undocu_p, .25),
    p50 = quantile(gbm_undocu_p, .50),
    p75 = quantile(gbm_undocu_p, .75),
    p90 = quantile(gbm_undocu_p, .90),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

kable(prob_distribution_check, digits = 4)
```

```{r}
# ============================================================================
# EXPORT ACS PREDICTIONS FOR STATA
# ============================================================================
# Export only the downstream GBM-named variables expected by the Stata pipeline.
# Even though the model is XGBoost, the variable names stay gbm_* so that the
# downstream Stata regression files do not need to change.
# ============================================================================

acs_gbm_export <- acs %>%
  mutate(
    gbm_high_prob = ifelse(gbm_undocu_q == "Q4", 1, 0),
    gbm_low_prob = ifelse(gbm_undocu_q == "Q1", 1, 0),
    gbm_high_recall = ifelse(gbm_undocu_p >= threshold_75_xgb, 1, 0)
  ) %>%
  select(
    gbm_undocu_p,
    gbm_undocu_q,
    gbm_high_prob,
    gbm_low_prob,
    gbm_high_recall,
    year,
    serial,
    pernum
  )

write_csv(
  acs_gbm_export,
  acs_prediction_export
)

tibble(
  output = "Official ACS prediction export for Stata",
  path = acs_prediction_export,
  rows = nrow(acs_gbm_export)
) %>%
  kable()
```

```{r}
# ============================================================================
# SAVE MODEL AND METADATA FOR REPRODUCIBILITY
# ============================================================================
# These files are optional but useful if later scripts need to load the exact
# trained model or threshold settings.
# ============================================================================

model_metadata <- list(
  model_name = "leading_xgboost",
  threshold_definition = "target-like SIPP test sample: college graduates, employed, ages 22-55",
  report_timestamp = report_timestamp,
  features = features,
  seed = seed,
  xgb_grid = xgb_grid,
  best_xgb = best_xgb,
  xgb_nrounds = xgb_nrounds,
  xgb_nfold = xgb_nfold,
  xgb_early_stopping_rounds = xgb_early_stopping_rounds,
  targetlike_breaks = xgb_targetlike_breaks,
  threshold_75_gbm = threshold_75_xgb,
  train_n = nrow(train_xgb),
  test_n = nrow(test_xgb),
  targetlike_test_n = nrow(sipp_targetlike_test),
  full_sipp_auc = as.numeric(auc_full),
  targetlike_sipp_auc = as.numeric(auc_targetlike),
  created_at = Sys.time()
)

saveRDS(xgb_model, leading_xgb_model_file)
saveRDS(model_metadata, leading_xgb_metadata_file)

tibble(
  object = c("Fitted leading XGBoost model", "Leading XGBoost metadata"),
  path = c(leading_xgb_model_file, leading_xgb_metadata_file)
) %>%
  kable()
```
